segments.s596-g89: جز از شهریارش نخوانیم کس is built around the negative verb نخوانیم (ن- + خوانیم), ‘we do not recount/mention’. جز + از introduces the exception phrase ‘except (from)’, and شهریارش contains the possessive enclitic -ش ‘his’ attached to شهریار (‘king’). کس (‘anyone’) follows the verb for emphasis under negation: literally, ‘we do not name anyone except his king.’
segments.s596-g90: ز گفتارها (poetic variant of از) means ‘from/regarding the speeches/tales’. داد here is the noun ‘justice’/‘due’, and دادیم is the finite verb ‘we gave/rendered’; the noun+verb pairing (داد دادیم) is a poetic way to say ‘we have rendered justice’ or ‘we have given what is due’ in the accounts. و بس (‘and enough’) closes the clause: ‘and that suffices.’
segments.s596-g41: نهفته‌مرا is built from نهفته (an adjective ‘hidden’) plus the possessive enclitic -م (my); را marks the following noun phrase as definite/object. گنج آگنده هست is the predication: گنج (treasure) modified by آگنده (filled/overflowing) followed by هست (copula/exists). Word order is emphatic/poetic: possessive adjective precedes the object marker and the predicate follows.
segments.s596-g42: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'the very' or 'those same' placed before the noun. نامداران is a plural substantive 'renowned ones' and خسروپرست is an epithet/composite adjective 'who worship Khosrow' placed after to qualify them; together they form an appositive description of the treasure's owners.
segments.s596-g43: جهان (the world) is the object/topic, modified by the adverbial intensifier یکسر 'entirely' and the adjective آباد 'prosperous' that together form یکسر آباد. دارم is first‑person present 'I have/hold'. به داد is a prepositional phrase 'by/through justice' specifying the means; here به takes the sense 'by'.
segments.s596-g44: شما is the addressee 'you'. یکسر آباد is the desiderative/adverbial phrase 'entirely prosperous'. باشید is a present subjunctive/optative (used as a wish/imperative) meaning 'may you be'; و connects to the following predicate شاد 'happy'.
segments.s596-g45: هران (each/every) modifies بوم (land, homeland). کز is the fused poetic form of the relative/causal 'که/از' meaning 'that/which/from which', introducing the subordinate clause. رنج ویران شدست is a passive/result clause: رنج (suffering) + ویران (ruined) + شدست (archaic perfect/past 'has become/was'), i.e. 'has been ruined by suffering.'
segments.s596-g46: ز is the poetic preposition meaning 'because of' or 'by'. بیدادی means 'injustice/cruelty' and names the cause. شاه ایران شدست is a verbless/passive‑style clause with شدست (has become/was) showing the result: the damage is attributed to 'the king of Iran's injustice.'
segments.s596-g47: من is the first‑person subject 'I'. آباد گردانم is a causative construction: آباد (prosperous) + گردانم (I make/turn/restore) — گردانم is first‑person singular present/future‑oriented form 'I cause/make'. آن را is the definite direct object 'that (place)', marked by را. به داد again indicates the means 'by/through justice'.
segments.s596-g48: همه زیردستان 'all the subjects' is the group referenced. بمانند is a third‑person plural subjunctive/optative form of ماندن used as a wish 'may they remain'. شاد is the predicative adjective 'happy'. The whole clause is a benedictory wish for the subjects' continued happiness.
segments.s596-g17: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic) governing یزدان (God) and combines with خواستم 'I asked/wished' to mean 'I asked of God'. همی is the archaic continuous/habitual marker placed before the verb to show a continued or habitual action ('have been asking' / 'always asked'). تاکنون is an adverb 'until now' modifying the whole verbal phrase and appears at the end for temporal scope.
segments.s596-g18: که introduces a subordinate clause of wish/purpose ('that'); باشد is 3rd-person singular subjunctive/optative of بودن used here like 'may he be' / 'that he be'. به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'in goodness' / 'well' modifying رهنمون. م + را represent the object 'm(a) + rā' (m = clitic for 'me', را = definite object marker), so 'مرا' = 'me' (object). رهنمون is a noun 'guide/guidance' functioning as the complement of باشد: 'that he be a good guide to me.'
segments.s596-g19: که تا together introduce a purposive/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'); تا here emphasizes purpose/result. هرچ is an indefinite/adverb ('whatever' / 'however'), followed by با مردمان 'with the people' (با = 'with'). کرد is the past tense verb 'did' and is placed before شاه (the subject) — classical Persian often places the verb before its subject, so 'کرد شاه' = 'the king did'.
segments.s596-g20: بشوییم is 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/optative of شستن ('may/we should wash' or 'let us wash/cleanse') expressing the intended/resultant action. ما is the explicit subject 'we' (here placed after the verb for emphasis). جان و دل is a fixed pairing 'soul and heart' (two objects united). زان is the compound 'from that/of that' (ز + ان = 'of/from that') referring back to the sin, and گناه is the noun 'sin' (object of cleansing).
segments.s596-g21: به کام دل is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/for the desire (of the) heart' — کام = 'desire/wish', دل = 'heart'. زیردستان is the plural noun 'subordinates/subjects' functioning as the possessor/agent whose hearts are meant. منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (من + ام = 'I am'), placed after the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s596-g22: بر آیین means 'according to/on the practice of' (بر = 'on/upon/toward', آیین = 'rite/custom/practice'). یزدان‌پرستان is the plural 'God-worshippers' / 'the pious' modifying آیین. The clause ends again with منم 'I am', repeating the copular identification 'I am one who follows that practice.'
segments.s596-g23: شبان is the predicate 'shepherd'. باشم is 1st-person singular present-subjunctive of بودن used here as a hortative/wish 'may I be / let me be'. و links the clauses. زیردستان رمه places the noun 'زیردستان' ('my subjects') next to رمه ('flock') in a simile/metaphor: 'and (may) my subjects (be) a flock' — the verb 'be' is understood from باشم and omitted before رمه.
segments.s596-g24: تن‌آسانی is a compound noun 'bodily comfort/ease'. و links two objects of desire. داد here is the noun 'justice' (not the past-tense verb 'gave'), and جویم is 1st-person singular present-subjunctive/optative of جویدن/جستن 'I seek' (hence 'I seek justice'). همه follows to indicate the scope 'for all/everyone' (it can modify the seeking: 'seek justice for all').
segments.s596-g9: رهانید is an imperative (release! — here plural/polite) directing someone to free something. طینوشم is طینا + ـ‎ش + من: 'my clay' (a possessive noun), and از دست اوی literally means 'from his hand/grasp' (از = from; دست = hand; اوی = his, a poetic possessive). The whole segment = 'Release my clay from his hand.'
segments.s596-g10: بشد is a past/resultative 'it became' (or 'it came to be'); خسته کام من is a noun phrase 'my weary/tired desire' (خسته = weary; کام من = my desire); از شست اوی = 'from his شست' (شست here = palm/thumb/touch) with اوی = 'his'. The clause says the speaker's desire was exhausted/wearied by his touch.
segments.s596-g11: ازان ('from that' / 'for that reason') introduces the motive; کرده‌ام is the perfect 'I have made/taken'; دست منذر پناه = 'the hand of Munḏir as refuge' (دست = hand/grasp, منذر here is the personal name Munḏir, پناه = refuge/protection). The clause means 'Because of that I have made Munḏir's hand my refuge.'
segments.s596-g12: که introduces the following explanatory clause 'for / because'; هرگز ندیدم = 'I have never seen' (negated perfect/past); نوازش ز شاه = 'kindness/affection from the king' (نوازش here = benevolent treatment). Together: 'for I have never seen kindness from the king.'
segments.s596-g13: بدان خو is 'to/into that habit' (ب = prefix 'to/into', دان here part of the deictic/verb complex forming 'to that', خو = habit/disposition). مبادا is a negative jussive 'may it not be / let it not happen'. که مردم بود introduces the content clause 'that one be (of) the people' — overall: 'May you not adopt that habit.'
segments.s596-g14: چو = 'when/if'; باشد here marks the circumstantial/present state 'is/comes to be'; پی + مردمی = 'in pursuit of / following the people' (پی = after/following, مردمی = of the people); گم بود = 'was lost' (گم = lost, بود = was). The clause states the consequence: 'when one follows the crowd, he is lost.'
segments.s596-g15: سپاسم ز یزدان = 'my thanks from God' (سپاس = thanks; ز یزدان = from God). که دارم خرد = 'that I have wisdom' (که introduces the content clause; دارم = I have; خرد = wisdom/intellect). The whole segment = 'I thank God that I possess wisdom.'
segments.s596-g16: روانم = 'my soul/spirit' (with the possessive -م); همی = an adverb 'always / continually'; از خرد برخورد = 'meets/encounters from wisdom' (از = from; خرد = wisdom; برخورد here = encounter/meeting). Read idiomatically: 'my spirit is always guided by / comes into contact with wisdom.'
segments.s596-g57: "شود" here functions as an auxiliary giving a future/optative sense (literally "may/shall be"), followed by the verb phrase "تاج برگیرد" (literally "take/assume the crown"). The prepositional phrase "از تخت عاج" (from the ivory throne) follows the verb phrase and marks the source. Note the classical Persian order: auxiliary + verb (subjunctive) phrase + complement.
segments.s596-g58: The phrase "به سر برنهد" is literal "place upon the head": "به" (onto/towards) + "سر" (head) + verb "برنهد" (to put/place on). "نامبردار تاج" is a post‑nominal descriptive phrase: "نامبردار" (renowned/the aforementioned) qualifies "تاج" (crown). The whole unit means he will set the (renowned) crown on his head; the verb is third person present/subjunctive used in this future/optative sense.
segments.s596-g59: "به شاهی نشیند" literally uses "به" + noun to indicate role or office ("into/for kingship") and the verb "نشیند" (to sit/take a seat) — together meaning "he will sit as king". "میان دو شیر" is a simple locative phrase "between two lions"; "میان" introduces the space and comes before the two nouns.
segments.s596-g60: This is an appositive/parallel locative clause: "میان شاه و تاج" = "between king and crown." The following short phrases "از بر و تخت زیر" are elliptical locatives: "از" (from/of), "بر" (on/above) and then "تخت زیر" (the throne beneath). Each word keeps its basic spatial sense; the line uses compact, poetic word order rather than a fully explicit clause.
segments.s596-g61: Word order is inverted for emphasis: "جز او را" = "except him" ("جز" + pronoun + object marker "را"). "نخواهیم کس پادشا" literally places the negated verb "نخواهیم" (we will not want) before "کس" (anyone/no one) and the predicate noun "پادشا" (king) follows, so: "We will want no one as king except him."
segments.s596-g62: "اگر" introduces a condition (if). "دادگر باشد" uses "باشد" (subjunctive/present of "to be") with the adjective "دادگر" (just), i.e. "if he is just." The conjunction "و" links the second adjective "پارسا" (pious), so the clause reads "if he is just and pious."
segments.s596-g63: "وگر" (but/if) introduces a counterfactual/alternative. "زین که گفتم" is a small relative phrase "the saddle that I mentioned/said" ("زین" = this/that saddle, "که گفتم" = which I said). The verb "بتابید" (here "bend/turn") governs "یال" (mane) as its subject: "if the mane turns/bends away from that saddle." Note the classical/poetic ordering keeps the relative clause tight with the noun it modifies.
segments.s596-g64: "گزینید" is an optative/imperative plural form meaning "choose/select (you)". "گردنکشی را" is the definite object marked by "را" ("the defiance/rebellion/obstinacy"), and "همال" (literally "bearer/carrier") follows as the noun that fills the role — "choose the bearer/carrier of defiance" (i.e. select someone who will carry/lead the obstinacy). The clause is an instruction in the imperative mood.
segments.s596-g25: منش هست is an existential/copiular phrase: منش (character) + هست (is/exists). The following nouns are simply coordinated with و (and) in a list: فرهنگ (culture), رای (counsel/opinion), و هنر (and virtue/skill). Word order is noun + هست (rather than a subject-verb order that would be more prosaic), a common poetic inversion.
segments.s596-g26: ندارد is the negative present third-person form of داشتن (to have) meaning 'does not have' or 'has none'. The object هنر appears before the subject شاه بیدادگر due to poetic inversion, so the clause reads literally 'has not art/virtue the tyrant-king' → 'the tyrant king has no virtue.'
segments.s596-g27: لئیمی و کژی are two coordinated nouns (baseness, crookedness). The preposition ز (from/because of) marks source, and بیچارگیست is the predicate: بیچارگی + است fused (is misery/comes from misery). The -ست ending is the poetic/contracted form of است.
segments.s596-g28: بباید + verb expresses necessity (archaic/poetic form of باید): بباید گریست = 'one must weep/one must lament'. به بیدادگر بر here frames the target of the lament: به ... بر ... combines to mean 'against/over the tyrant' (i.e., to lament the tyrant). The verb گریست is used in the sense 'lament/weep'.
segments.s596-g29: پدر بر پدر is a poetic expression meaning 'from father to father' or 'through my fathers/ancestors' (a marker of lineage). پادشاهی is the noun 'kingship' and مراست here is the fused poetic form of مرا است (belongs to me / is mine). The line thus states that kingship, by ancestral right, is mine.
segments.s596-g30: خردمندی و نیکخواهی are coordinated abstract nouns (wisdom and benevolence). Again مراست (poetic contraction of مرا است) predicates possession: 'are mine' or 'belong to me.' This repeats the claim of personal entitlement to these virtues.
segments.s596-g31: ز marks the starting point 'from' and introduces a sequence of royal names (شاپور، بهرام) followed by تا (until) and another name (اردشیر): 'from Shapur and Bahram to Ardashir'. This is a standard poetic way to list a span of rulers.
segments.s596-g32: همه شهریاران is 'all the kings/rulers' (همه = all). برنا و پیر are coordinated adjectives 'young and old' describing those rulers. Word order is adjective after noun, a common poetic placement.
segments.s596-g1: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the past tense 'said' (poetic word order often puts verb before subject: 'چنین گفت بهرام' = 'thus said Bahram'). کای is a vocative particle ('O') placed before the addressee, and مهتران is a vocative plural 'chiefs/elders' following کای.
segments.s596-g2: جهاندیده and کارکرده are two adjectival/participial modifiers joined by و ('and') that precede and modify سران. The phrase literally lists qualities ('worldly‑wise and having acted/experienced') that characterize the leaders (سران) — standard adjective + noun order in poetic Persian.
segments.s596-g3: همه راست گفتید is 'you all spoke truly' (همه = everyone/all; راست = true; گفتید = 2nd‑person plural past 'you said/spoke' — poetic). و زین بترست is an idiomatic/archaic phrase: زین = 'from/ because of this' and بترست here is an archaic contraction of بهتر است ('it is better'); thus 'and for this it is better...' (not a literal 'fear'). Note: the provided sense list does not include the contracted/archaic sense, so some sense indexes are unavailable.
segments.s596-g4: پدر را is the definite object ('the father') marked by را. نکوهش کنم is a verbal expression meaning 'I should reproach' or 'I ought to blame' (نکوهش = reproach; کنم is the present/subjunctive/optative form of کردن used to form the verbal phrase). در خورست is an idiomatic fused phrase meaning 'it is fitting' or 'worthy' (در خورِ است → درخورست) — here stating that reproaching the father is fitting.
segments.s596-g5: ازین (از + این) here functions as 'this'/'from this' modifying چاشنی ('seasoning' or figurative 'temptation/appeal'). هست is the copula 'is', and نزدیک من ('near me') is the locative complement. Word order places the copula between noun and its locative complement: 'This seasoning is near me.'
segments.s596-g6: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which / from it'. تیره شد is the past 'became dark/gloomy' (تیره = dark; شد = became). رای تاریک من is the noun phrase 'my dark counsel/opinion' with رای ('opinion, counsel') modified by تاریک ('dark') and possessed by من ('my'); Persian poetic word order often places the verb before the possessed noun ('from which became dark my counsel').
segments.s596-g7: چو is a subordinating particle meaning 'when/as'. ایوان او is 'his iwan/porch/hall' (noun + possessive pronoun), بود is the past copula 'was', and زندان من is 'my prison' (noun + possessive). The clause reads 'When his iwan was my prison' with the typical poetic inversion.
segments.s596-g8: چو again = 'when'. بخشایش (forgiveness) is the object, آورد is the past 'brought/caused', and یزدان من is 'my God' or more idiomatically 'God (for) me' — here the subject (یزدان) and the dative/possessive (من) appear after the verb in poetic order, yielding 'when God brought forgiveness to me.'
segments.s596-g33: پدر بر پدربر نیای منند functions as a nominal clause naming the lineage: پدر (father) + بر (literally “on/over,” here joining elements in a poetic piling-up) + پدربر (paternal/fatherly, an attributive form) + نیای (ancestor) with منند (مَانَند / مانند) meaning “are like / are.” In effect the phrase says ‘fathers and fatherly forebears are (my) ancestors’; the copula is supplied by منند at the end.
segments.s596-g34: به دین و خرد رهنمای منند: به introduces the domain (‘in, with regard to’), دین و خرد is a coordinated phrase ‘religion and wisdom’, and رهنمای (رهنما with the possessive/attributive vowel) is the noun ‘guide’; منند again supplies the copula ‘are/are like’. So literally ‘they are guides in religion and wisdom.’
segments.s596-g35: ز مادر نبیره شمیران شهم: ز (from) marks origin; مادر (mother) is the source; نبیره (scion, offspring) + شمیران (the place-name modifying نبیره) forms ‘a scion of Shemiran’; شهم is an appositive describing that scion as a noble/hero. The subject is understood rather than explicitly conjugated.
segments.s596-g36: ز هر گوهری با خرد همرهم: ز هر گوهری ('from/of every jewel') is idiomatic for ‘possessing every precious thing’; با خرد ('with wisdom') states accompaniment, and همرهم is همره with the enclitic -م (my) so ‘wisdom is my companion.’ Note the pronominal suffix -م on همرهم marking the first person.
segments.s596-g37: هنر هم خرد هم بزرگیم هست uses هم ... هم ... as the correlative ‘both ... and ...’; هنر (skill/virtue) and خرد (wisdom) are parallel, بزرگیم is بزرگي + -م ‘my greatness/ greatness (with first‑person suffix)’, and هست is the copula/existential verb ‘is/there is’. The clause asserts possession/existence of these qualities.
segments.s596-g38: سواری و مردی و نیروی دست is an asyndetic list (linked by و) that specifies the kinds of excellence just named: سواری (horsemanship/riding), مردی (manliness/valor), and نیرویِ دست (lit. ‘strength of hand’ = physical/hand strength). These nouns complete the predication begun with هست in the previous segment.
segments.s596-g39: کسی را ندارم ز مردان به مرد: کسی را ندارم is a fixed negative construction ‘I have no one / I lack anyone’ (را marks کسی as the definite object). ز مردان = ‘among/from the men’ narrows the domain, and به مرد is comparative/qualificatory ‘equal to a man’ (here idiomatically ‘as manly/valiant’). The whole means ‘there is no one among men who equals (me) in manliness.’
segments.s596-g40: به رزم و به بزم و به هر کارکرد repeats به before each noun for emphasis: ‘in battle, in the banquet/feast, and in every action/enterprise.’ Each prepositional phrase specifies fields in which no equal is found; کارکرد here denotes ‘deed/undertaking/performance.’
segments.s596-g81: A short clause headed by the temporal adverb کنون ('now'). آنک is a relative/introductory particle ('that/what') introducing reported speech; گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (past), with او 'he' as the subject. The preposition ز introduces the phrase ز شیر ژیان ('of/from the lion‑hearted'), where شیر ژیان is a poetic epithet ('lion' + 'life' = 'lion‑hearted/brave'). Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic (verb گفت before the prepositional phrase).
segments.s596-g82: An appositive/continuation of what was said: یکی ('one' as a numeral) + a compound noun تاج و تخت کیی ('crown and Kayanian throne'). کیی is an epithet (Kayanian) modifying the nouns. بر میان is a short prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'in the midst' (بر + میان, an archaic/poetic order equivalent to modern در میان). The whole nominal string is elliptical (no explicit verb in this segment).
segments.s596-g83: A protasis introduced by the conditional particle گر ('if'). او را is the definite object (را marks the object 'him'); بدرند is a 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive/optative form of بریدن ('to tear') used here in a conditional sense. شیران نر ('male lions') is the agent; the noun نر postmodifies شیران (poetic order places the subject after the verb for emphasis).
segments.s596-g84: Result clause: ز خونش ('concerning/from his blood') is a prepositional phrase functioning as the object of بپرسد. بپرسد is a 3rd‑person present/subjunctive/predictive form of پرسیدن ('to ask'); here it means 'will ask' or 'would ask.' The second ز (ز ما) means 'of/from us' and دادگر ('the just one'/'just ruler') is the person who will perform the asking (either subject or vocative depending on scansion). Note the doubled ز: the first attaches to خونش, the second to ما.
segments.s596-g85: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when.' خود is emphatic/reflexive ('he himself'). گفت is the verb 'said' (past). The coordinating و joins the next clause: این رسم بد خود نهاد — literally 'he placed/established this bad custom on himself.' نهاد is the simple past (3rd sg.) of نهادن 'to place/establish' and خود after بد is a reflexive object 'upon himself.' Word order is typical of elevated/poetic Persian with objects/prepositional phrases placed before the verb.
segments.s596-g86: Relative clause started by همان کز ('the very thing that'); کز is the contracted/poetic form of که ز ('that/from which'). به مرگش ('at his death' / 'on his death') is a temporal/prepositional phrase. نباشیم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive/optative negative of بودن ('we should not be'/'we shall not be'), followed by the predicate adjective شاد ('happy/joyful'). The clause expresses that they would not rejoice at his death.
segments.s596-g87: Another conditional introduced by ور ('if'). ایدون is an archaic/poetic present/subjunctive form meaning 'he comes' or 'if he should come.' کجا ('where/anywhere') is adverbial; تاج is the object 'the crown.' بردارد is a 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of برداشتن ('to take/lift'); اوی is an emphatic pronoun 'he.' The sequence expresses a hypothetical: 'if he should come and take the crown.'
segments.s596-g88: The result clause: به فر از فریدون گذر دارد اوی. به فر ('by/with the farr') places 'farr' (royal glory) as the relevant quality; از فریدون ('from Fereydun') specifies the source. گذر دارد is a verbal compound 'has/possesses a passage/portion/claim' (گذر + دارد), with دارد as the present tense auxiliary 'has' and اوی again as emphatic subject 'he.' Poetic word order puts oblique phrases before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s596-g73: This is a sequence of short actions in narrative (verb‑first, subject omitted): بگفت is an archaic third‑person past 'he said'; این points to the preceding words ('this'); و simply links actions ('and/then'); برخاست is simple past 'rose/got up'; the following و again links; در is the preposition 'in/into' governing خیمه 'the tent'; شد here is the copular/auxiliary used with a locative phrase to mean 'went/ended up (in)' or 'was (inside)'.
segments.s596-g74: جهانی functions as a collective 'the whole world / all present'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by' introducing cause; گفتارش is 'his speech' (گفتار + possessive suffix -ش); آسیمه is an adjectival predicate 'distraught/agitated'; شد is the verb 'became' (past) combining with آسیمه to mean 'was thrown into confusion'.
segments.s596-g75: This clause sets the scene of scope and participants: به ایران + رد expresses 'throughout/through Iran' (رد as 'passage/throughout'); و links to موبدان 'the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)'; هرک is an indefinite relative 'whoever/whichever (was present)'; بود is the past copula 'was' introducing the relative clause that follows.
segments.s596-g76: که is the relative particle 'who/that'; the phrase گفتار آن شاه دانا places the object ('the speech') before its possessor ('that wise king')—a common poetic order—and شنود is an archaic third‑person form 'heard'/'hears' used here as the verb of the relative clause.
segments.s596-g77: بگفتند is the perfect/simple past 'they said' (or 'it was said'); کین here is a demonstrative ('this/that') in older usage (does not match the provided nominal senses); فره is the subject 'farr' (divine glory); ایزدیست is a predicative adjective + copula 'is divine/from God' (ایزدی + -ست).
segments.s596-g78: نه negates the following attribution ('not'); از راه is an idiom 'from the way/path of' where راه = 'way/manner'; کژی و نابخردیست lists the two kinds (kaji = 'crookedness/wrongness', نابخردی = 'folly') with نابخردیست as predicate 'is folly'—together rendering 'not from crookedness or folly.'
segments.s596-g79: نگوید همی is a classical negative imperative or prohibitive expression ('do not speak' / 'let no one say'), with همی adding an emphatic/continuous sense ('at all/always'); یک سخن جز به داد means 'not a single word except with/on the subject of داد (justice)'. The clause therefore commands: say nothing except in praise/defense of justice.
segments.s596-g80: سزدگر is an agentive gerund/participle 'one to whom it is fitting' (literally 'fitting‑maker' = 'it is fitting'); دل 'heart' is the subject; از داد 'because of/from justice' gives the cause; داریم شاد is a verbless/result clause in which داریم 'we have/possess' + شاد 'joyful' means 'our heart is glad' (we are glad). Together: 'for if the heart rejoices because of justice, that is fitting.'
segments.s596-g49: یکی is an indefinite/introductory particle here (literally 'one') used idiomatically like English 'let me' or 'I will (do this once)'; با شما ('with you') is a prepositional phrase marking the partner of the pact; نیز ('also') adds inclusion/emphasis. پیمان is the direct object 'pact/covenant' and کنم is the 1st-person singular present-subjunctive/jussive of کردن ('to make/do'), used here to express the speaker's intention or request ('let me make' / 'I will make').
segments.s596-g50: زبان را is the definite object (زبان = 'speech/tongue'; را marks it as definite/accusative). به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'to/for God'. گروگان‌کنم is 'I will make (it) a hostage/pledge' — گروگان is the noun 'hostage/pledge' and کنم is again 1st-person singular subjunctive/jussive of کردن, giving the sense 'I will make (my tongue) a hostage (to God)'. The clause therefore expresses a solemn pledge (swearing by God) in a literal, somewhat poetic phrasing.
segments.s596-g51: بیاریم is a 1st-person plural present-subjunctive / hortative form (from آوردن) meaning 'let us bring' / 'we will bring' (poetic usage). شاهنشهی qualifies تخت and means 'imperial' / 'of kingship', so شاهنشهی تخت عاج is an inverted adjective-noun phrase meaning 'the imperial ivory throne' (عاج = 'ivory', تخت = 'throne'). Poetic word order allows the modifier(s) to appear before the head noun.
segments.s596-g52: برش here functions as 'on/for it' (بر + ضمیر -ش) referring back to the throne ('on it' / 'for it'); در میان means 'in the middle/center'; تنگ is used adverbially 'tightly/firmly' or as 'narrow' indicating a close fit; بنهیم is the 1st-person plural present-subjunctive of نهادن 'to put/place' (we shall place); تاج is the object 'crown'. Together: 'we shall place the crown firmly in its center.' Note: classic/poetic Persian allows the pronominal clitic and prepositional phrases to appear before the verb.
segments.s596-g53: ز (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'from'; بیشه = 'grove/thicket' is the source; دو شیر ژیان = 'two living / vigorous lions' where ژیان ('life, living') adjectivally qualifies شیر; آوریم is 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/hortative of آوردن 'to bring' ('we will bring' / 'let us bring'). The clause literally: 'We will bring two living lions from the grove.'
segments.s596-g54: همان is a demonstrative 'that very' / 'the same', qualifying تاج 'crown'; را marks تاج as definite object. در میان آوریم is the verb phrase 'we bring/put into the middle' (آوریم again = 'we bring/place'), so the whole segment means 'we place that same crown into the middle.' Word order here is poetic but the object is clearly the crown (را) and آوریم is the action.
segments.s596-g55: ببندیم is the 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive of بستن ('to bind/fasten') meaning 'let us bind' / 'we will bind'. شیر ژیان is the noun phrase 'the living / fierce lions' (object). بر دو سوی is a locative phrase 'on both sides' (بر = 'on', دو سوی = 'two sides'); placement of this prepositional phrase after the object is a permitted poetic/inverted order in Persian.
segments.s596-g56: کسی را is the definite/indefinite object marked with را ('someone' as the person concerned). که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; شاهی is 'kingship' (a noun) and کند is the 3rd-person singular present-subjunctive/indicative of کردن ('does/makes'), here functioning with آرزوی (a noun 'wish') to mean 'who wishes'—i.e. آرزوی ... کند = 'wishes for ...'. Put together: '... on either side of anyone who wishes kingship.' The verb کند governs the clause 'آرزوی شاهی' (to desire kingship).
segments.s596-g65: به جایی که is a locative phrase: به here marks direction/location ('to/toward') and جایی is the noun 'a place'; که introduces a relative clause ('where/that'). چون من بود is a relative clause meaning 'where one like me was' or 'as I was', with چون = 'as/like' and بود the past copula 'was'. پیش رو is a postpositive adverbial phrase meaning 'in front / before (me)', formed from پیش 'in front' + رو 'toward/face'.
segments.s596-g66: This unit names the subject and its predicate: سنان سواران (spear(s) and riders) is the nominal subject and بود is the past copula 'were/was'. خار و خو is the predicate complement describing them; خار is used metaphorically as 'sharp/thorny' and خو 'disposition/temper', joined by و 'and'.
segments.s596-g67: A coordinated list functioning as the grammatical subject: من و منذر و گرز و شمشیر تیز = 'I and Munḏir and (the) mace and (the) sharp sword.' تیز is an adjective modifying شمشیر ('sharp sword'). The series is emphatic and personifies the weapons alongside the men.
segments.s596-g68: ندانند is the negative present/past-form of دانستن here meaning 'they/do not know' (classical narrative usage; negative prefix ن- plus دانند). گردان تازی گریز is the object phrase 'flight from the Arab troop' (گردان تازی = 'Arab troop', گریز = 'flight/escape'); word order places the object phrase after the verb 'do not know'. The sense is boastful: 'we do not know how to flee the Arab host.'
segments.s596-g69: برآریم is a first-person-plural hortative/optative form 'we will raise / let us raise' (verb + suffix). گرد here means 'dust' (metaphorical commotion) and functions as the direct object; از introduces the source/target 'from/against your emperor' (شهنشاهتان = 'your king of kings/sovereign').
segments.s596-g70: همان 'the same/that' picks up 'the same action' and از marks 'from/of'. بر و بوم is an idiomatic pairing meaning 'land and dwelling / homeland', with بر here part of the fixed phrase; وز (here written as one word) functions as a compound 'و ز' = 'and from' introducing گاهتان ('your time(s)' or 'your season(s)'). The whole clause extends the scope: 'and similarly from your lands and homes and from your times.'
segments.s596-g71: کنون is the adverb 'now'. آنچ گفتیم is a noun phrase 'that which we said' (آنچ = 'that which'), with گفتیم the past verb 'we said' referring back to preceding speech. پاسخ دهید is an imperative plural 'give an answer' (پاسخ = 'answer', دهید = 'give'); the object 'that which we said' precedes the imperative in this classical order.
segments.s596-g72: بدین داوری is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this judgment' (بدین = به + این). رای فرخ is a noun phrase 'an auspicious/favorable opinion/decision' (رای modified by فرخ 'fortunate/auspicious'). نهید here is a 2nd-person-plural form of نهادن 'to place/set' used as an injunction/subjunctive 'put/place (this)', so the whole clause urges 'place this auspicious judgment'—i.e. render a favorable decision.

sentences.s596-s45: We name no one except his king; as for the tales, we have rendered justice — and that is enough.
sentences.s596-s21: A hidden, overflowing treasure is mine — those very renowned, Khosrow‑worshipping men.
sentences.s596-s22: I hold the whole world prosperous by justice; may you be entirely prosperous and happy.
sentences.s596-s23: Every land that has been laid waste by suffering — it has been ruined by the injustice of the king of Iran.
sentences.s596-s24: I will make it prosperous by justice; may all the subjects remain happy.
sentences.s596-s9: I have always asked God until now that He be my guide in goodness.
sentences.s596-s10: so that, whatever the king did with the people, we may wash our life and heart of that sin.
sentences.s596-s11: I am for the satisfaction of my subjects' hearts; I am in the custom/practice of the God-worshippers.
sentences.s596-s12: May I be a shepherd, and my subjects a flock; I seek bodily ease and seek justice for all.
sentences.s596-s5: Free my clay from his hand; my desire became weary from his touch.
sentences.s596-s6: For that reason I have taken Munḏir's hand as my refuge, for I never saw any kindness from the king.
sentences.s596-s7: May you not take that habit, for when one follows the crowd he is lost.
sentences.s596-s8: I thank God that I have wisdom; my spirit always encounters (is guided by) wisdom.
sentences.s596-s29: He will take the crown from the ivory throne and place that famed crown upon his head.
sentences.s596-s30: He will sit as king between two lions; between the king and the crown, with the throne beneath.
sentences.s596-s31: We will want no one as king except him, if he is just and pious.
sentences.s596-s32: But if the mane I mentioned should turn away from that saddle, choose the bearer of defiance.
sentences.s596-s13: There is character, culture, counsel and virtue; the tyrant king has no virtue.
sentences.s596-s14: Baseness and crookedness come from misery; one must lament the tyrant.
sentences.s596-s15: Kingship from father to father is mine; wisdom and goodwill are mine.
sentences.s596-s16: From Shapur and Bahram to Ardashir, all the kings, young and old,
sentences.s596-s1: Thus Bahram said, "O chiefs, worldly‑wise and experienced leaders."
sentences.s596-s2: You have all spoken truly, and because of this it is better that I reproach my father — it is fitting.
sentences.s596-s3: This seasoning is near me; from it my counsel became dark.
sentences.s596-s4: When his iwan was my prison, when God brought forgiveness to me.
sentences.s596-s17: Fathers and forefathers are my ancestors; they are my guides in religion and wisdom.
sentences.s596-s18: From my mother I am a scion of Shemiran, a noble; with every jewel and wisdom as my companion.
sentences.s596-s19: I have skill, wisdom, and greatness — horsemanship, manliness, and the strength of the hand.
sentences.s596-s20: I have no one among men equal to a man in battle, in the feast, or in any undertaking.
sentences.s596-s41: Now, what he said of the lion‑hearted: one crown and the Kayanian throne in the middle.
sentences.s596-s42: If the male lions should tear him apart, the just one will ask us about his blood.
sentences.s596-s43: When he himself said this and set this evil custom upon himself — the very thing that we should not be glad of at his death.
sentences.s596-s44: If he were to come and take the crown anywhere, he would possess the farr (royal glory) that comes from Fereydun.
sentences.s596-s37: He said this, rose, and went into the tent; all present were thrown into confusion by his words.
sentences.s596-s38: Throughout Iran and among the mobeds, whoever was there heard the speech of that wise king.
sentences.s596-s39: They said, 'This farr (divine glory) is from God, not from crookedness or folly.'
sentences.s596-s40: Speak not a single word except for justice; for if from justice the heart is glad, it is fitting.
sentences.s596-s25: Let me also make a pact with you; I will give my tongue to God as a hostage.
sentences.s596-s26: Let us bring the imperial ivory throne; we shall set the crown firmly in its center.
sentences.s596-s27: From the grove we will bring two living lions, and we will place that same crown in the middle between them.
sentences.s596-s28: We will fasten the living lions on both sides of anyone who desires kingship.
sentences.s596-s33: To a place where, before me, spears and horsemen stood—sharp and fierce.
sentences.s596-s34: Munḏir and I—mace and the sharp sword—do not know to flee the Arab host (i.e., we do not know flight from the Arab troop).
sentences.s596-s35: We will raise a dust about your sovereign, and likewise upon your land and homes and from your seasons.
sentences.s596-s36: Now answer what we have said; render a favorable judgment accordingly.
